markdown: , digits:4) = {¥n  let rounded-data = data.map(¥n    row => row.map(¥n      value => calc.round(value, digits: digits)¥n    )¥n  )¥n¥n  math.mat(..rounded-data, delim:delim)¥n}¥n¥n#let scalar_matrix_mult(scalar, matrix) = {¥n  matrix.map(¥n    row => row.map(¥n      v => v * scalar¥n    )¥n  )¥n}¥n¥n#let r4(v) = {¥n  calc.round(v, digits: 4)¥n}¥n¥n#let bmat(..args) = math.mat(..args, delim: ¥

)¥n¥n¥n```¥n¥n```{=typst}¥n¥nA flat metallic plate with length 100 mm, width 40 mm, and thickness 5 mm is subjected to a uniform tensile load. When the axial force was measured as 20 kN the axial elongation was 0.1 mm and in-plane lateral contraction 0.008 mm. Determine the corresponding constitutive matrix *D* for a plane stress 2D linear elastic model.¥n¥n== Solution¥n¥n#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.1.5.#n)], supplement: none)¥n¥n#let L = 100 / 1000 // m¥n#let width = 40 / 1000 // m¥n#let thicc = 5 / 1000 // m¥n#let F = 20e3 // N¥n#let d_axial = 0.1e-3 // m¥n#let d_lateral = -0.008e-3 // m¥n¥n¥nFirstly, we need to calculate the tensions¥n#let axial_area = width * thicc¥n#let sigma_x = F / axial_area¥n$ sigma_x = F / A = (#F ¥
$¥n¥nIt is known that $E = sigma / epsilon$, so:¥n¥n#let E = sigma_x / epsilon_x ¥n¥n$ E = #sigma_x / #epsilon_x = #E ¥

$¥n¥n#let epsilon_y = d_lateral / width¥n¥nSimilarly¥n#let poisson = -(epsilon_y / epsilon_x)¥n¥n$ epsilon_y = (Delta W) / (W) = (#d_lateral) / (#width) = #calc.round( epsilon_y, digits: 4) $¥n¥n$ nu = - epsilon_y / epsilon_x = #calc.round(epsilon_y, digits: 4) / #epsilon_x = #calc.round(poisson, digits:4) $¥n¥nThe constitutive Matrix is then defined by¥n¥n$ bold(D) = E / (1 - nu^2) dot mat(1, nu, 0; nu, 1, 0; 0, 0, (1-nu)/2) $¥n¥nso¥n$ bold(D) = #E / (1 - (#calc.round(poisson, digits: 4))^2)¥n¥ndot¥n¥nmat(delim: ¥

,¥n1, #calc.round(poisson, digits:4), 0;¥n#calc.round(poisson, digits:4), 1, 0;¥n0, 0, (1 - (#calc.round(poisson, digits:4))) / (2)¥n¥n) $¥n¥n#let mult = E / (1 - poisson * poisson)¥n#let m = (¥n  (1, poisson, 0),¥n  (poisson, 1, 0),¥n  (0, 0, (1 - poisson) / 2)¥n)¥n#let array2mat(data, delim:¥

, digits:4) = {¥n  let rounded-data = data.map(¥n    row => row.map(¥n      value => calc.round(value, digits: digits)¥n    )¥n  )¥n¥n  math.mat(..rounded-data, delim:delim)¥n}¥n¥n#let r4(v) = {¥n  calc.round(v, digits: 4)¥n}¥n¥n$ bold(D) = #r4(mult * 1e-9)¥

$¥n¥n```¥n---¥n¥n¥n# Exercise 4.1.6¥n¥n```{=typst}¥n¥n¥n#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.1.6.#n)], supplement: none)¥n¥nA cylindrical concrete sample with height 20 cm and diameter 10 cm is tested in uniaxial compression. At a point within the elastic range, the measured values are: axial stress 20 MPa, axial shortening 0.2 mm, and radial expansion 0.015 mm. Using these measurements, determine the elastic parameters E and ﾎｽ, and construct the corresponding constitutive matrix 僩足 for a plane stress linear #footnote[I've read the problem incorrectly and done also the axissimetrical development, oops!] elastic model. ¥n¥n¥n¥n¥n== Solution¥n¥n#let height_cm = 20¥n#let diameter_cm = 10¥n#let sigma_z_MPa = 20¥n#let delta_z_mm = -.2 // compression so negative¥n#let delta_r_mm = .015 ¥n¥n// Determine E and v¥n¥nFirstly, we need to define Young's module:¥n¥n$ E = sigma / epsilon $ <a1>¥n¥n$ epsilon = (Delta L) / L $ <a2>¥n¥nSubstituting @a2 in @a1, we get¥n¥n#let E_MPa = sigma_z_MPa * height_cm / (calc.abs(delta_z_mm / 10))¥n¥n$ E = (sigma dot L) / (Delta L) = (#sigma_z_MPa ¥

$¥n¥nNow, we may obtain epsilon in relation to the radius:¥n#let epsilon_r = (delta_r_mm * 1e-3) / (diameter_cm / 2 * 1e-2)¥n¥n#let epsilon_z = (delta_z_mm * 1e-3) / (height_cm * 1e-2)¥n¥n$ epsilon_z = (Delta L) / L = #epsilon_z ¥

$¥n¥nDefining Poisson:¥n#let poisson = -(epsilon_r / epsilon_z)¥n¥n$ nu = - epsilon_r / epsilon_z = #poisson $¥n¥nFirstly, let's analyse the constitutive matrix for an axissimetrical element, defined as¥n¥n$ bmat(sigma_¥

;epsilon_(theta theta); gamma_(r z))¥n$¥n¥nWhere *D* is defined by¥n¥n$¥nbold(D) =¥nE / ((1 + nu)(1 - 2nu))¥ndot¥nbmat(¥n  1-nu, nu, nu, 0;¥n  nu, 1-nu, nu, 0;¥n  nu, nu, 1-nu, 0;¥n  0, 0, 0, (1-2nu)/2¥n)¥n$¥n¥n#let mult_MPa = E_MPa / ((1 + poisson)*(1 - 2 * poisson)) ¥n#let get_m(nu) = {¥n  (¥n    (1-nu, nu, nu, 0),¥n    (nu, 1-nu, nu, 0),¥n    (nu, nu, 1-nu, 0),¥n    (0, 0, 0, (1-2*nu)/2)¥n  )¥n}¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D)¥n  =¥n  #r4(mult_MPa) ¥
¥n  dot¥n  #array2mat(get_m(poisson))¥n$¥n¥n$¥n  =¥n  #array2mat(digits:1,¥n    get_m(poisson).map(¥n      row => row.map(¥n        v => v * mult_MPa¥n      )¥n    )¥n  ) ¥

¥n$¥n¥nFinally, to define the same element in a plane stress linear elastic model:¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D) =¥n  E / (1 - nu^2)¥n  mat(¥n    1, nu, 0;¥n    nu, 1, 0;¥n    0, 0, (1 - nu) / 2¥n  )¥n$¥n¥n#let plane_stress(nu) = {¥n  (¥n    (1, nu, 0),¥n    (nu, 1, 0),¥n    (0, 0, (1-nu)/2),¥n  )¥n}¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D) = ¥n  (#E_MPa ¥

) / (1 - #(poisson)^2) dot¥n  #array2mat(¥n    plane_stress(poisson)¥n  )¥n$¥n¥n#let mult_plane_stress_MPa = E_MPa / (1 - poisson * poisson)¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D) = ¥n  #array2mat(¥n    plane_stress(poisson).map(¥n      row => row.map(¥n        value => value * mult_plane_stress_MPa / 10000¥n      )¥n    )¥n  )¥n  dot 10^4¥n  ¥

¥n$¥n¥n¥n```¥n¥n---¥n¥n# Exercise 4.1.7¥n¥n```{=typst}¥n¥n¥nA long thick-walled cylindrical pipe with inner radius 僩綜 = 50 mm and outer radius 僩聡 = 100 mm is subjected to a uniform internal pressure 僩増 = 10 MPa. The material is linear elastic and isotropic, with Young窶冱 modulus 僩精 = 200 GPa and Poisson窶冱 ratio 僩怦 = 0.2. Assume that the pipe is sufficiently long so that the axial strain can be neglected, i.e. 僩怙僩息 = 0. At a point located at 僩臓 = 75 mm within the wall, the stress state is characterized by normal stresses only, with no shear components. Determine the stress components at this point and the corresponding strain components.¥n¥n== Solution¥n¥n#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.1.7.#n)], supplement: none)¥n¥nSince the internal pressure acts in the radial direction, and assuming axial strength can be neglected, it is valid to assume that the problem constitutes an Axisymmetric Stress, defined by¥n¥n$¥n  bold(sigma) =¥n  bmat(¥n    sigma_(r r), 0, sigma_(r z);¥n    0, sigma_(theta theta), 0;¥n    sigma_(z r), 0, sigma_(z z)¥n  )¥n$¥n¥nIt is known, for the current material, that¥n¥n#let poisson = .2¥n#let E_MPa = 200¥n¥n$ nu = 0.2 $¥n¥n$ E = 200 ¥
;epsilon_(theta theta); gamma_(r z))¥n$¥n¥nBut, since axial strain can be neglected, that is, $epsilon_(z z) = 0$, we can define it as¥n¥n¥n$ bmat(sigma_¥

;epsilon_(theta theta); gamma_(r z))¥n$¥n¥nWhere $bold(D)$, in this specific case, will be¥n¥n#let axyssimetrical_matrix(nu) = {¥n  (¥n    (1-nu, nu, 0),¥n    (nu, 1-nu, 0),¥n    (0, 0, (1-2*nu)/2)¥n  )¥n}¥n¥n$¥nbold(D) =¥nE / ((1 + nu)(1 - 2nu))¥ndot¥nbmat(¥n  1-nu, nu, 0;¥n  nu, 1-nu, 0;¥n  0, 0, (1-2nu)/2¥n)¥n$¥n¥n#let axys = axyssimetrical_matrix(poisson)¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D) =¥n  #E_MPa / ((1 + #poisson)(1 - #(2*poisson))) dot¥n  #array2mat(¥n    axys¥n  )¥n$¥n¥n#let mult_axy_MPa = E_MPa / (1 + poisson) / (1 - 2* poisson)¥n¥n#let D_mat_MPa = scalar_matrix_mult(mult_axy_MPa, axys)¥n¥n$¥n  bold(D) =¥n  #array2mat(D_mat_MPa) ¥
¥n$ ¥n¥nWith the defined matrix:#footnote[This problem is incomplete]¥n¥n¥n¥n¥n```
